mappings.rationaleAI: Prehistory spanning four million years provides context for prehistoric religion (A) and religions attributed to various cultural stages (A.3).
Archaeology provides methods of interpretation for studying prehistoric religion (A.1).
Human evolution provides the biological framework for prehistoric religion (A.1) and cultural stages (A.3).
Anthropological study of belief systems provides frameworks for primitive religion (B.1), primitive views of reality (B.2), and myth and symbol (B.3).
Indigenous African religions including ancestor worship (B.4.d), rites of passage, and divination relate to primitive religious practices (B.4) and African peoples (B.5.a).
Indigenous North American spiritual traditions address religions of the Americas (B.5.c).
Aztec religious practices including sacrifice relate to religions of the Americas (B.5.c).
Ritual including rites of passage and sacrifice relates to primitive religious practices (B.4) and sacrifice and passage rites (B.4.a).
Theoretical approaches to myth relate to myth and symbol in primitive religion (B.3).
Comparative mythology relates to myth and symbol in primitive religion (B.3).
Magic relates to shamanism and sorcery (A.2.f) and divination practices (B.4.e).
Witchcraft relates to sorcery and magic (A.2.f) and shamanism and divination (B.4.e).
Pagan beliefs relate to primitive religion (B), its significance (B.1), and shamanism (B.4.e).
Druids practicing sacrifice (A.2.c), sorcery (A.2.f), and divination (B.4.e).
Superstitious beliefs relate to divination and spiritualistic practices (B.4.e).
Celtic religious traditions relate to aboriginal religions (B.5.d).
Mesopotamian religious institutions provide context for early cultural stages (A.3).
Ice age environmental history provides context for prehistoric cultural stages (A.3) and study methods (A.1).
